testsuites.name: Dastardly scan results - have a vulnerability-free day!

testcase.name: Cross-origin resource sharing

failure.message: Cross-origin resource sharing found at http://host.docker.internal:3000/socket.io/

testcase.failure:
Severity: Info
Confidence: Certain
Host: http://host.docker.internal:3000
Path: /socket.io/
Issue Detail The application implements an HTML5 cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) policy for this request.
If the application relies on network firewalls or other IP-based access controls, this policy is likely to present a security risk.
Issue Background An HTML5 cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) policy controls whether and how content running on other domains can perform two-way interaction with the domain that publishes the policy. The policy is fine-grained and can apply access controls per-request based on the URL and other features of the request. If another domain is allowed by the policy, then that domain can potentially attack users of the application. If a user is logged in to the application, and visits a domain allowed by the policy, then any malicious content running on that domain can potentially retrieve content from the application, and sometimes carry out actions within the security context of the logged in user.
Even if an allowed domain is not overtly malicious in itself, security vulnerabilities within that domain could potentially be leveraged by an attacker to exploit the trust relationship and attack the application that allows access. CORS policies on pages containing sensitive information should be reviewed to determine whether it is appropriate for the application to trust both the intentions and security posture of any domains granted access.
Issue Remediation Any inappropriate domains should be removed from the CORS policy.
Evidence Request: POST /socket.io/?EIO=4&transport=polling&t=Py2remD&sid=_1GaSfB9Ka12nfcwAAIE HTTP/1.1 Host: host.docker.internal:3000 Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate, br Accept: */* Accept-Language: en-US;q=0.9,en;q=0.8 User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/134.0.0.0 Safari/537.36 Connection: close Cache-Control: max-age=0 Origin: http://host.docker.internal:3000 Content-type: text/plain;charset=UTF-8 Referer: http://host.docker.internal:3000/ Sec-CH-UA: "Google Chrome";v="134", "Not=A?Brand";v="8", "Chromium";v="134" Sec-CH-UA-Platform: "Linux" Sec-CH-UA-Mobile: ?0 Content-Length: 2
40
Response: HTTP/1.1 400 Bad Request Access-Control-Allow-Origin: http://localhost:4200 Vary: Origin Content-Type: application/json Date: Fri, 26 Jun 2026 07:32:03 GMT Connection: close Content-Length: 41
{"code":1,"message":"Session ID unknown"}
References
- Web Security Academy: Cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) (https://portswigger.net/web-security/cors) - Exploiting CORS Misconfigurations (https://portswigger.net/research/exploiting-cors-misconfigurations-for-bitcoins-and-bounties)
Vulnerability Classifications
- CWE-942: Overly Permissive Cross-domain Whitelist (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/942.html)
Reported by Dastardly: https://portswigger.net/burp/dastardly/scan-checks

testcase.name: HTML does not specify charset

failure.message: HTML does not specify charset found at http://host.docker.internal:3000/socket.io/

testcase.failure:
Severity: Info
Confidence: Certain
Host: http://host.docker.internal:3000
Path: /socket.io/
Issue Description If a response states that it contains HTML content but does not specify a character set, then the browser may analyze the HTML and attempt to determine which character set it appears to be using. Even if the majority of the HTML actually employs a standard character set such as UTF-8, the presence of non-standard characters anywhere in the response may cause the browser to interpret the content using a different character set. This can have unexpected results, and can lead to cross-site scripting vulnerabilities in which non-standard encodings like UTF-7 can be used to bypass the application's defensive filters.
In most cases, the absence of a charset directive does not constitute a security flaw, particularly if the response contains static content. You should review the contents of affected responses, and the context in which they appear, to determine whether any vulnerability exists.
Issue Remediation For every response containing HTML content, the application should include within the Content-type header a directive specifying a standard recognized character set, for example charset=ISO-8859-1.
Evidence Request: POST /socket.io/?EIO=4&transport=polling&t=Py2qwlO&sid=ivNDHDX2riP9ZsbzAACm HTTP/1.1 Host: host.docker.internal:3000 Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate, br Accept: */* Accept-Language: en-US;q=0.9,en;q=0.8 User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/134.0.0.0 Safari/537.36 Connection: close Cache-Control: max-age=0 Origin: http://host.docker.internal:3000 Content-type: text/plain;charset=UTF-8 Referer: http://host.docker.internal:3000/ Sec-CH-UA: "Google Chrome";v="134", "Not=A?Brand";v="8", "Chromium";v="134" Sec-CH-UA-Platform: "Linux" Sec-CH-UA-Mobile: ?0 Content-Length: 2
40
Response: HTTP/1.1 200 OK Access-Control-Allow-Origin: http://localhost:4200 Vary: Origin Content-Type: text/html Content-Length: 2 Date: Fri, 26 Jun 2026 07:27:34 GMT Connection: close
ok
Vulnerability Classifications
- CWE-16: Configuration (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/16.html) - CWE-436: Interpretation Conflict (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/436.html)
Reported by Dastardly: https://portswigger.net/burp/dastardly/scan-checks

testcase.name: Cross-origin resource sharing - arbitrary origin trusted

failure.message: Cross-origin resource sharing: arbitrary origin trusted found at http://host.docker.internal:3000/media/il-HFTY4SM2.svg

testcase.failure:
Severity: Info
Confidence: Certain
Host: http://host.docker.internal:3000
Path: /media/il-HFTY4SM2.svg
Issue Detail The application implements an HTML5 cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) policy for this request that allows access from any domain.
The application allowed access from the requested origin http://wxhxerrqbsux.com
If the application relies on network firewalls or other IP-based access controls, this policy is likely to present a security risk.
Since the Vary: Origin header was not present in the response, reverse proxies and intermediate servers may cache it. This may enable an attacker to carry out cache poisoning attacks.
Issue Background An HTML5 cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) policy controls whether and how content running on other domains can perform two-way interaction with the domain that publishes the policy. The policy is fine-grained and can apply access controls per-request based on the URL and other features of the request.
Trusting arbitrary origins effectively disables the same-origin policy, allowing two-way interaction by third-party web sites. Unless the response consists only of unprotected public content, this policy is likely to present a security risk.
If the site specifies the header Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true, third-party sites may be able to carry out privileged actions and retrieve sensitive information. Even if it does not, attackers may be able to bypass any IP-based access controls by proxying through users' browsers.
Issue Remediation Rather than using a wildcard or programmatically verifying supplied origins, use a whitelist of trusted domains.
Evidence Request: GET /media/il-HFTY4SM2.svg HTTP/1.1 Host: host.docker.internal:3000 Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate, br Accept: image/avif,image/webp,image/apng,image/svg+xml,image/*,*/*;q=0.8 Accept-Language: en-US;q=0.9,en;q=0.8 User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/134.0.0.0 Safari/537.36 Connection: close Cache-Control: max-age=0 Cookie: language=en Referer: http://host.docker.internal:3000/styles.css Sec-CH-UA: "Google Chrome";v="134", "Not=A?Brand";v="8", "Chromium";v="134" Sec-CH-UA-Platform: "Linux" Sec-CH-UA-Mobile: ?0 Origin: http://wxhxerrqbsux.com
Response: HTTP/1.1 200 OK Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff X-Frame-Options: SAMEORIGIN Feature-Policy: payment 'self' X-Recruiting: /#/jobs Accept-Ranges: bytes Cache-Control: public, max-age=0 Last-Modified: Tue, 12 May 2026 12:02:15 GMT ETag: W/"386-19e1c10f958" Content-Type: image/svg+xml Content-Length: 902 Vary: Accept-Encoding Date: Fri, 26 Jun 2026 07:31:40 GMT Connection: close
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" id="flag-icons-il" viewBox="0 0 640 480"> <defs> <clipPath id="il-a"> <path fill-opacity=".7" d="M-87.6 0H595v512H-87.6z"/> </clipPath> </defsSnip
References
- Web Security Academy: Cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) (https://portswigger.net/web-security/cors) - Exploiting CORS Misconfigurations (https://portswigger.net/research/exploiting-cors-misconfigurations-for-bitcoins-and-bounties)
Vulnerability Classifications
- CWE-942: Overly Permissive Cross-domain Whitelist (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/942.html)
Reported by Dastardly: https://portswigger.net/burp/dastardly/scan-checks

testcase.name: Cross-origin resource sharing

failure.message: Cross-origin resource sharing found at http://host.docker.internal:3000/media/cn-P7M3XOME.svg

testcase.failure:
Severity: Info
Confidence: Certain
Host: http://host.docker.internal:3000
Path: /media/cn-P7M3XOME.svg
Issue Detail The application implements an HTML5 cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) policy for this request.
If the application relies on network firewalls or other IP-based access controls, this policy is likely to present a security risk.
Since the Vary: Origin header was not present in the response, reverse proxies and intermediate servers may cache it. This may enable an attacker to carry out cache poisoning attacks.
Issue Background An HTML5 cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) policy controls whether and how content running on other domains can perform two-way interaction with the domain that publishes the policy. The policy is fine-grained and can apply access controls per-request based on the URL and other features of the request. If another domain is allowed by the policy, then that domain can potentially attack users of the application. If a user is logged in to the application, and visits a domain allowed by the policy, then any malicious content running on that domain can potentially retrieve content from the application, and sometimes carry out actions within the security context of the logged in user.
Even if an allowed domain is not overtly malicious in itself, security vulnerabilities within that domain could potentially be leveraged by an attacker to exploit the trust relationship and attack the application that allows access. CORS policies on pages containing sensitive information should be reviewed to determine whether it is appropriate for the application to trust both the intentions and security posture of any domains granted access.
Issue Remediation Any inappropriate domains should be removed from the CORS policy.
Evidence Request: GET /media/cn-P7M3XOME.svg HTTP/1.1 Host: host.docker.internal:3000 Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate, br Accept: image/avif,image/webp,image/apng,image/svg+xml,image/*,*/*;q=0.8 Accept-Language: en-US;q=0.9,en;q=0.8 User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/134.0.0.0 Safari/537.36 Connection: close Cache-Control: max-age=0 Cookie: language=en Referer: http://host.docker.internal:3000/styles.css Sec-CH-UA: "Google Chrome";v="134", "Not=A?Brand";v="8", "Chromium";v="134" Sec-CH-UA-Platform: "Linux" Sec-CH-UA-Mobile: ?0 Origin: http://host.docker.internal:3000
Response: HTTP/1.1 200 OK Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff X-Frame-Options: SAMEORIGIN Feature-Policy: payment 'self' X-Recruiting: /#/jobs Accept-Ranges: bytes Cache-Control: public, max-age=0 Last-Modified: Tue, 12 May 2026 12:02:15 GMT ETag: W/"32d-19e1c10f958" Content-Type: image/svg+xml Content-Length: 813 Vary: Accept-Encoding Date: Fri, 26 Jun 2026 07:31:40 GMT Connection: close

testcase.failure:
Severity: Info
Confidence: Certain
Host: http://host.docker.internal:3000
Path: /media/de-UZ33VXB7.svg
Issue Detail The application implements an HTML5 cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) policy for this request that allows access from any domain.
The application allowed access from the requested origin http://cbqkcqyskfni.com
If the application relies on network firewalls or other IP-based access controls, this policy is likely to present a security risk.
Since the Vary: Origin header was not present in the response, reverse proxies and intermediate servers may cache it. This may enable an attacker to carry out cache poisoning attacks.
Issue Background An HTML5 cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) policy controls whether and how content running on other domains can perform two-way interaction with the domain that publishes the policy. The policy is fine-grained and can apply access controls per-request based on the URL and other features of the request.
Trusting arbitrary origins effectively disables the same-origin policy, allowing two-way interaction by third-party web sites. Unless the response consists only of unprotected public content, this policy is likely to present a security risk.
If the site specifies the header Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true, third-party sites may be able to carry out privileged actions and retrieve sensitive information. Even if it does not, attackers may be able to bypass any IP-based access controls by proxying through users' browsers.
Issue Remediation Rather than using a wildcard or programmatically verifying supplied origins, use a whitelist of trusted domains.

Reported by Dastardly: https://portswigger.net/burp/dastardly/scan-checks

testcase.name: Cross-origin resource sharing - arbitrary origin trusted

failure.message: Cross-origin resource sharing: arbitrary origin trusted found at http://host.docker.internal:3000/media/cz-PSDLWGGB.svg

testcase.failure:
Severity: Info
Confidence: Certain
Host: http://host.docker.internal:3000
Path: /media/cz-PSDLWGGB.svg
Issue Detail The application implements an HTML5 cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) policy for this request that allows access from any domain.
The application allowed access from the requested origin http://plmkpydikepk.com
If the application relies on network firewalls or other IP-based access controls, this policy is likely to present a security risk.
Since the Vary: Origin header was not present in the response, reverse proxies and intermediate servers may cache it. This may enable an attacker to carry out cache poisoning attacks.
Issue Background An HTML5 cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) policy controls whether and how content running on other domains can perform two-way interaction with the domain that publishes the policy. The policy is fine-grained and can apply access controls per-request based on the URL and other features of the request.
Trusting arbitrary origins effectively disables the same-origin policy, allowing two-way interaction by third-party web sites. Unless the response consists only of unprotected public content, this policy is likely to present a security risk.
If the site specifies the header Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true, third-party sites may be able to carry out privileged actions and retrieve sensitive information. Even if it does not, attackers may be able to bypass any IP-based access controls by proxying through users' browsers.
Issue Remediation Rather than using a wildcard or programmatically verifying supplied origins, use a whitelist of trusted domains.
Evidence Request: GET /media/cz-PSDLWGGB.svg HTTP/1.1 Host: host.docker.internal:3000 Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate, br Accept: image/avif,image/webp,image/apng,image/svg+xml,image/*,*/*;q=0.8 Accept-Language: en-US;q=0.9,en;q=0.8 User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/134.0.0.0 Safari/537.36 Connection: close Cache-Control: max-age=0 Cookie: language=en Referer: http://host.docker.internal:3000/styles.css Sec-CH-UA: "Google Chrome";v="134", "Not=A?Brand";v="8", "Chromium";v="134" Sec-CH-UA-Platform: "Linux" Sec-CH-UA-Mobile: ?0 Origin: http://plmkpydikepk.com
Response: HTTP/1.1 200 OK Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff X-Frame-Options: SAMEORIGIN Feature-Policy: payment 'self' X-Recruiting: /#/jobs Accept-Ranges: bytes Cache-Control: public, max-age=0 Last-Modified: Tue, 12 May 2026 12:02:15 GMT ETag: W/"e1-19e1c10f958" Content-Type: image/svg+xml Content-Length: 225 Vary: Accept-Encoding Date: Fri, 26 Jun 2026 07:34:25 GMT Connection: close
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" id="flag-icons-cz" viewBox="0 0 640 480"> <path fill="#fff" d="M0 0h640v240H0z"/> <path fill="#d7141a" d="M0 240h640v240H0z"/> <path fill="#11457e" d="M36Snip

testcase.name: Cross-origin resource sharing

failure.message: Cross-origin resource sharing found at http://host.docker.internal:3000/media/cz-PSDLWGGB.svg

testcase.failure:
Severity: Info
Confidence: Certain
Host: http://host.docker.internal:3000
Path: /media/cz-PSDLWGGB.svg
Issue Detail The application implements an HTML5 cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) policy for this request.
If the application relies on network firewalls or other IP-based access controls, this policy is likely to present a security risk.
Since the Vary: Origin header was not present in the response, reverse proxies and intermediate servers may cache it. This may enable an attacker to carry out cache poisoning attacks.
Issue Background An HTML5 cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) policy controls whether and how content running on other domains can perform two-way interaction with the domain that publishes the policy. The policy is fine-grained and can apply access controls per-request based on the URL and other features of the request. If another domain is allowed by the policy, then that domain can potentially attack users of the application. If a user is logged in to the application, and visits a domain allowed by the policy, then any malicious content running on that domain can potentially retrieve content from the application, and sometimes carry out actions within the security context of the logged in user.
Even if an allowed domain is not overtly malicious in itself, security vulnerabilities within that domain could potentially be leveraged by an attacker to exploit the trust relationship and attack the application that allows access. CORS policies on pages containing sensitive information should be reviewed to determine whether it is appropriate for the application to trust both the intentions and security posture of any domains granted access.
Issue Remediation Any inappropriate domains should be removed from the CORS policy.
Evidence Request: GET /media/cz-PSDLWGGB.svg HTTP/1.1 Host: host.docker.internal:3000 Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate, br Accept: image/avif,image/webp,image/apng,image/svg+xml,image/*,*/*;q=0.8 Accept-Language: en-US;q=0.9,en;q=0.8 User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/134.0.0.0 Safari/537.36 Connection: close Cache-Control: max-age=0 Cookie: language=en Referer: http://host.docker.internal:3000/styles.css Sec-CH-UA: "Google Chrome";v="134", "Not=A?Brand";v="8", "Chromium";v="134" Sec-CH-UA-Platform: "Linux" Sec-CH-UA-Mobile: ?0 Origin: http://host.docker.internal:3000
Response: HTTP/1.1 200 OK Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff X-Frame-Options: SAMEORIGIN Feature-Policy: payment 'self' X-Recruiting: /#/jobs Accept-Ranges: bytes Cache-Control: public, max-age=0 Last-Modified: Tue, 12 May 2026 12:02:15 GMT ETag: W/"e1-19e1c10f958" Content-Type: image/svg+xml Content-Length: 225 Vary: Accept-Encoding Date: Fri, 26 Jun 2026 07:34:25 GMT Connection: close

Vulnerability Classifications
- CWE-942: Overly Permissive Cross-domain Whitelist (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/942.html)

testcase.name: Cross-origin resource sharing - arbitrary origin trusted

failure.message: Cross-origin resource sharing: arbitrary origin trusted found at http://host.docker.internal:3000/api/Feedbacks/

testcase.failure:
Severity: Info
Confidence: Certain
Host: http://host.docker.internal:3000
Path: /api/Feedbacks/
Issue Detail The application implements an HTML5 cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) policy for this request that allows access from any domain.
The application allowed access from the requested origin http://wbbfjhgifwvw.com
If the application relies on network firewalls or other IP-based access controls, this policy is likely to present a security risk.
Since the Vary: Origin header was not present in the response, reverse proxies and intermediate servers may cache it. This may enable an attacker to carry out cache poisoning attacks.
Issue Background An HTML5 cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) policy controls whether and how content running on other domains can perform two-way interaction with the domain that publishes the policy. The policy is fine-grained and can apply access controls per-request based on the URL and other features of the request.
Trusting arbitrary origins effectively disables the same-origin policy, allowing two-way interaction by third-party web sites. Unless the response consists only of unprotected public content, this policy is likely to present a security risk.
If the site specifies the header Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true, third-party sites may be able to carry out privileged actions and retrieve sensitive information. Even if it does not, attackers may be able to bypass any IP-based access controls by proxying through users' browsers.
Issue Remediation Rather than using a wildcard or programmatically verifying supplied origins, use a whitelist of trusted domains.
Evidence Request: GET /api/Feedbacks/ HTTP/1.1 Host: host.docker.internal:3000 Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate, br Accept: application/json, text/plain, */* Accept-Language: en-US;q=0.9,en;q=0.8 User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/134.0.0.0 Safari/537.36 Connection: close Cache-Control: max-age=0 Cookie: language=en Referer: http://host.docker.internal:3000/ Sec-CH-UA: "Google Chrome";v="134", "Not=A?Brand";v="8", "Chromium";v="134" Sec-CH-UA-Platform: "Linux" Sec-CH-UA-Mobile: ?0 Origin: http://wbbfjhgifwvw.com
Response: HTTP/1.1 200 OK Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff X-Frame-Options: SAMEORIGIN Feature-Policy: payment 'self' X-Recruiting: /#/jobs Content-Type: application/json; charset=utf-8 ETag: W/"6c6-RI+KeT/lLDoEgmCZo/FzJleO66w" Vary: Accept-Encoding Date: Fri, 26 Jun 2026 07:32:09 GMT Connection: close Content-Length: 1734
{"status":"success","data":[{"UserId":1,"id":1,"comment":"I love this shop! Best products in town! Highly recommended! (***in@juice-sh.op)","rating":5,"createdAt":"2026-06-26T06:54:16.672Z","updatedAtSnip

Vulnerability Classifications
- CWE-942: Overly Permissive Cross-domain Whitelist (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/942.html)
Reported by Dastardly: https://portswigger.net/burp/dastardly/scan-checks

testcase.name: Cross-origin resource sharing

failure.message: Cross-origin resource sharing found at http://host.docker.internal:3000/api/Feedbacks/

testcase.failure:
Severity: Info
Confidence: Certain
Host: http://host.docker.internal:3000
Path: /api/Feedbacks/
Issue Detail The application implements an HTML5 cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) policy for this request.
If the application relies on network firewalls or other IP-based access controls, this policy is likely to present a security risk.
Since the Vary: Origin header was not present in the response, reverse proxies and intermediate servers may cache it. This may enable an attacker to carry out cache poisoning attacks.
Issue Background An HTML5 cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) policy controls whether and how content running on other domains can perform two-way interaction with the domain that publishes the policy. The policy is fine-grained and can apply access controls per-request based on the URL and other features of the request. If another domain is allowed by the policy, then that domain can potentially attack users of the application. If a user is logged in to the application, and visits a domain allowed by the policy, then any malicious content running on that domain can potentially retrieve content from the application, and sometimes carry out actions within the security context of the logged in user.
Even if an allowed domain is not overtly malicious in itself, security vulnerabilities within that domain could potentially be leveraged by an attacker to exploit the trust relationship and attack the application that allows access. CORS policies on pages containing sensitive information should be reviewed to determine whether it is appropriate for the application to trust both the intentions and security posture of any domains granted access.
Issue Remediation Any inappropriate domains should be removed from the CORS policy.
Evidence Request: GET /api/Feedbacks/ HTTP/1.1 Host: host.docker.internal:3000 Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate, br Accept: application/json, text/plain, */* Accept-Language: en-US;q=0.9,en;q=0.8 User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/134.0.0.0 Safari/537.36 Connection: close Cache-Control: max-age=0 Cookie: language=en Referer: http://host.docker.internal:3000/ Sec-CH-UA: "Google Chrome";v="134", "Not=A?Brand";v="8", "Chromium";v="134" Sec-CH-UA-Platform: "Linux" Sec-CH-UA-Mobile: ?0 Origin: http://host.docker.internal:3000
Response: HTTP/1.1 200 OK Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff X-Frame-Options: SAMEORIGIN Feature-Policy: payment 'self' X-Recruiting: /#/jobs Content-Type: application/json; charset=utf-8 ETag: W/"6c6-RI+KeT/lLDoEgmCZo/FzJleO66w" Vary: Accept-Encoding Date: Fri, 26 Jun 2026 07:32:05 GMT Connection: close Content-Length: 1734

testcase.name: Cross-origin resource sharing - arbitrary origin trusted

failure.message: Cross-origin resource sharing: arbitrary origin trusted found at http://host.docker.internal:3000/assets/public/images/JuiceShop_Logo.png

testcase.failure:
Severity: Info
Confidence: Certain
Host: http://host.docker.internal:3000
Path: /assets/public/images/JuiceShop_Logo.png
Issue Detail The application implements an HTML5 cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) policy for this request that allows access from any domain.
The application allowed access from the requested origin http://zyyegsqqiioa.com
If the application relies on network firewalls or other IP-based access controls, this policy is likely to present a security risk.
Since the Vary: Origin header was not present in the response, reverse proxies and intermediate servers may cache it. This may enable an attacker to carry out cache poisoning attacks.
Issue Background An HTML5 cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) policy controls whether and how content running on other domains can perform two-way interaction with the domain that publishes the policy. The policy is fine-grained and can apply access controls per-request based on the URL and other features of the request.
Trusting arbitrary origins effectively disables the same-origin policy, allowing two-way interaction by third-party web sites. Unless the response consists only of unprotected public content, this policy is likely to present a security risk.
If the site specifies the header Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true, third-party sites may be able to carry out privileged actions and retrieve sensitive information. Even if it does not, attackers may be able to bypass any IP-based access controls by proxying through users' browsers.
Issue Remediation Rather than using a wildcard or programmatically verifying supplied origins, use a whitelist of trusted domains.
Evidence Request: GET /assets/public/images/JuiceShop_Logo.png HTTP/1.1 Host: host.docker.internal:3000 Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate, br Accept: image/avif,image/webp,image/apng,image/svg+xml,image/*,*/*;q=0.8 Accept-Language: en-US;q=0.9,en;q=0.8 User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/134.0.0.0 Safari/537.36 Connection: close Cache-Control: max-age=0 Referer: http://host.docker.internal:3000/ Sec-CH-UA: "Google Chrome";v="134", "Not=A?Brand";v="8", "Chromium";v="134" Sec-CH-UA-Platform: "Linux" Sec-CH-UA-Mobile: ?0 Origin: http://zyyegsqqiioa.com
Response: HTTP/1.1 200 OK Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff X-Frame-Options: SAMEORIGIN Feature-Policy: payment 'self' X-Recruiting: /#/jobs Accept-Ranges: bytes Cache-Control: public, max-age=0 Last-Modified: Tue, 12 May 2026 11:59:39 GMT ETag: W/"2255e-19e1c0e97f8" Content-Type: image/png Content-Length: 140638 Date: Fri, 26 Jun 2026 07:31:51 GMT Connection: close

testcase.name: Cross-origin resource sharing

failure.message: Cross-origin resource sharing found at http://host.docker.internal:3000/assets/public/images/JuiceShop_Logo.png

testcase.failure:
Severity: Info
Confidence: Certain
Host: http://host.docker.internal:3000
Path: /assets/public/images/JuiceShop_Logo.png
Issue Detail The application implements an HTML5 cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) policy for this request.
If the application relies on network firewalls or other IP-based access controls, this policy is likely to present a security risk.
Since the Vary: Origin header was not present in the response, reverse proxies and intermediate servers may cache it. This may enable an attacker to carry out cache poisoning attacks.
Issue Background An HTML5 cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) policy controls whether and how content running on other domains can perform two-way interaction with the domain that publishes the policy. The policy is fine-grained and can apply access controls per-request based on the URL and other features of the request. If another domain is allowed by the policy, then that domain can potentially attack users of the application. If a user is logged in to the application, and visits a domain allowed by the policy, then any malicious content running on that domain can potentially retrieve content from the application, and sometimes carry out actions within the security context of the logged in user.
Even if an allowed domain is not overtly malicious in itself, security vulnerabilities within that domain could potentially be leveraged by an attacker to exploit the trust relationship and attack the application that allows access. CORS policies on pages containing sensitive information should be reviewed to determine whether it is appropriate for the application to trust both the intentions and security posture of any domains granted access.
Issue Remediation Any inappropriate domains should be removed from the CORS policy.
Evidence Request: GET /assets/public/images/JuiceShop_Logo.png HTTP/1.1 Host: host.docker.internal:3000 Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate, br Accept: image/avif,image/webp,image/apng,image/svg+xml,image/*,*/*;q=0.8 Accept-Language: en-US;q=0.9,en;q=0.8 User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/134.0.0.0 Safari/537.36 Connection: close Cache-Control: max-age=0 Referer: http://host.docker.internal:3000/ Sec-CH-UA: "Google Chrome";v="134", "Not=A?Brand";v="8", "Chromium";v="134" Sec-CH-UA-Platform: "Linux" Sec-CH-UA-Mobile: ?0 Origin: http://host.docker.internal:3000

testcase.name: Cross-origin resource sharing - arbitrary origin trusted

failure.message: Cross-origin resource sharing: arbitrary origin trusted found at http://host.docker.internal:3000/about.component-DCTXVOS4.js

testcase.failure:
Severity: Info
Confidence: Certain
Host: http://host.docker.internal:3000
Path: /about.component-DCTXVOS4.js
Issue Detail The application implements an HTML5 cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) policy for this request that allows access from any domain.
The application allowed access from the requested origin http://qibiqhlesxnd.com
If the application relies on network firewalls or other IP-based access controls, this policy is likely to present a security risk.
Since the Vary: Origin header was not present in the response, reverse proxies and intermediate servers may cache it. This may enable an attacker to carry out cache poisoning attacks.
Issue Background An HTML5 cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) policy controls whether and how content running on other domains can perform two-way interaction with the domain that publishes the policy. The policy is fine-grained and can apply access controls per-request based on the URL and other features of the request.
Trusting arbitrary origins effectively disables the same-origin policy, allowing two-way interaction by third-party web sites. Unless the response consists only of unprotected public content, this policy is likely to present a security risk.
If the site specifies the header Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true, third-party sites may be able to carry out privileged actions and retrieve sensitive information. Even if it does not, attackers may be able to bypass any IP-based access controls by proxying through users' browsers.
Issue Remediation Rather than using a wildcard or programmatically verifying supplied origins, use a whitelist of trusted domains.

testcase.name: Cross-origin resource sharing

failure.message: Cross-origin resource sharing found at http://host.docker.internal:3000/about.component-DCTXVOS4.js

testcase.failure:
Severity: Info
Confidence: Certain
Host: http://host.docker.internal:3000
Path: /about.component-DCTXVOS4.js
Issue Detail The application implements an HTML5 cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) policy for this request.
If the application relies on network firewalls or other IP-based access controls, this policy is likely to present a security risk.
Since the Vary: Origin header was not present in the response, reverse proxies and intermediate servers may cache it. This may enable an attacker to carry out cache poisoning attacks.
Issue Background An HTML5 cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) policy controls whether and how content running on other domains can perform two-way interaction with the domain that publishes the policy. The policy is fine-grained and can apply access controls per-request based on the URL and other features of the request. If another domain is allowed by the policy, then that domain can potentially attack users of the application. If a user is logged in to the application, and visits a domain allowed by the policy, then any malicious content running on that domain can potentially retrieve content from the application, and sometimes carry out actions within the security context of the logged in user.
Even if an allowed domain is not overtly malicious in itself, security vulnerabilities within that domain could potentially be leveraged by an attacker to exploit the trust relationship and attack the application that allows access. CORS policies on pages containing sensitive information should be reviewed to determine whether it is appropriate for the application to trust both the intentions and security posture of any domains granted access.
Issue Remediation Any inappropriate domains should be removed from the CORS policy.
